_readme: Third practice import pack — 14 problems covering patterns the first two packs miss (linked-list, trees, dp-1d/dp-2d, backtracking, intervals, monotonic-stack, math, two-pointers/hard) and harder SQL (self-join, subqueries, window functions). POST to /api/admin/practice/problems with an admin bearer token (see seed/README-practice-seed.md). DSA = stdin->stdout judged via Piston; SQL = in-app sql.js judged against expectedColumns/expectedRows. SQL problems ask the solver to ALIAS columns so result names match expectedColumns.

problems.title: Reverse Linked List
problems.statementHtml:
You are given a singly linked list of n integer values. Print the values of the reversed list, space-separated.

Input
Line 1: n.
Line 2: n space-separated integers — the list from head to tail.

Output
The reversed list, space-separated on a single line.

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0])
vals = list(map(int, d[1:1+n]))
# TODO: print the reversed list, space-separated

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();long[] a=new long[n];for(int i=0;i<n;i++)a[i]=s.nextLong();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Iterate once, tracking a `prev` pointer; flip each `next` to point to `prev`.
Or just reverse the array of values — the linked-list framing is a tutorial.

problems.editorialHtml: Either iterate pointers (classic linked-list reversal, O(n) / O(1)) or reverse the array of values. Both are O(n).

solutions.code:
import sys
d=sys.stdin.read().split()
n=int(d[0]);vals=list(map(int,d[1:1+n]))
print(' '.join(map(str, reversed(vals))))

problems.title: Tree Height (Parent Array)
problems.statementHtml:
A rooted tree on n nodes (numbered 0..n-1) is given as a parent array. Node 0 is the root. For each non-root node i, parent[i] is given (with the guarantee parent[i] < i). Print the height of the tree — the number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path (a single-node tree has height 1, an empty tree has height 0).

Input
Line 1: n.
Line 2 (only when n > 1): n-1 integers — parent[1], parent[2], …, parent[n-1].

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0])
parents = list(map(int, d[1:n]))   # length n-1
# TODO: print the height (0 if n == 0)

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();int[] p=new int[Math.max(0,n-1)];for(int i=0;i<p.length;i++)p[i]=s.nextInt();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Because parent[i] < i, you can compute depth[i] = depth[parent[i]] + 1 in a single forward pass.

problems.editorialHtml: depth[0] = 1; for i in 1..n-1, depth[i] = depth[parent[i]] + 1. Answer is max(depth) (or 0 if n == 0). O(n).

problems.title: Climbing Stairs
problems.statementHtml: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can climb either 1 or 2 stairs. Print the number of distinct ways to reach the top.
problems.constraintsHtml:
• 1 ≤ n ≤ 45
  • The answer fits in a 64-bit integer.

starters.code:
n = int(input())
# TODO: print the number of ways

const n = parseInt(require('fs').readFileSync(0,'utf8').trim());
// TODO: print the number of ways

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);long n=s.nextLong();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-2). It's the Fibonacci sequence.

problems.editorialHtml: Roll two variables: a, b = 1, 1; repeat a, b = b, a + b n times; print a. O(n) time, O(1) space.

solutions.code:
n=int(input())
a,b=1,1
for _ in range(n): a,b=b,a+b
print(a)

problems.title: Reverse Integer
problems.statementHtml: Given a signed 32-bit integer x, print the integer obtained by reversing its decimal digits (preserving the sign). If the reversed value falls outside the signed 32-bit range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1], print 0.

starters.code:
x = int(input())
# TODO: print the reversed integer, or 0 on 32-bit overflow

const x = parseInt(require('fs').readFileSync(0,'utf8').trim());
// TODO: print the reversed integer, or 0 on 32-bit overflow

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);long x=s.nextLong();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Strip the sign, reverse the digits as a string or by repeated %10/÷10, then re-apply the sign.
Check against 2^31 - 1 (= 2147483647) and -2^31 (= -2147483648) before printing.

problems.editorialHtml: Reverse via repeated %10/÷10 (or string reverse), reapply the sign, and bounds-check against the 32-bit signed range.

solutions.code:
x=int(input())
sign=-1 if x<0 else 1
r=int(str(abs(x))[::-1])*sign
print(r if -2**31<=r<=2**31-1 else 0)

problems.title: Generate Parentheses
problems.statementHtml: Given n, print every well-formed string of n pairs of parentheses — one combination per line, in lexicographic order (the order produced by adding '(' before ')' at each step).

starters.code:
n = int(input())
# TODO: print every well-formed string of n pairs, one per line, in lexicographic order

const n = parseInt(require('fs').readFileSync(0,'utf8').trim());
// TODO: print every well-formed string of n pairs, one per line, in lexicographic order

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Backtrack with counters: you can add '(' while open < n, and ')' while close < open.
Trying '(' before ')' at each step naturally produces lexicographic order.

problems.editorialHtml: Recursive backtracking with (open, close) counters. Always try '(' before ')'. Total combinations = Catalan number C(n).

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.statementHtml:
You are given n closed intervals [a, b]. Merge every set of overlapping (or touching) intervals and print the resulting intervals, one per line, in ascending order of start. Two intervals [a, b] and [c, d] overlap if a ≤ d AND c ≤ b.

Input
Line 1: n.
Next n lines: two integers a b.

Output
Each merged interval on its own line: a b.

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0])
intervals = [(int(d[1+2*i]), int(d[2+2*i])) for i in range(n)]
# TODO: print merged intervals, one per line

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();long[][] a=new long[n][2];for(int i=0;i<n;i++){a[i][0]=s.nextLong();a[i][1]=s.nextLong();}/* TODO */}}

testCases.explanation: [1,3] and [2,6] overlap → [1,6]; the rest are disjoint.

hints.text: Sort intervals by start, then sweep — extend the current merged end when the next start ≤ current end.

problems.editorialHtml: Sort by start (O(n log n)), then sweep once with a running merged interval. Emit it whenever the next start exceeds the current end.

problems.title: Unique Paths
problems.statementHtml:
A robot is on an m × n grid, starting at the top-left cell (0, 0). At each step it can move only right or down. Print the number of unique paths to the bottom-right cell (m-1, n-1).

Input
A single line with two integers m and n.
problems.constraintsHtml:
• 1 ≤ m, n ≤ 100
  • The answer fits in a signed 64-bit integer.

starters.code:
m, n = map(int, input().split())
# TODO: print the number of unique paths

const [m, n] = require('fs').readFileSync(0,'utf8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number);
// TODO: print the number of unique paths

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int m=s.nextInt(),n=s.nextInt();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Or use the closed-form: C(m + n - 2, m - 1).

problems.editorialHtml: 2-D DP in O(m·n), or the combinatorial formula C(m + n - 2, m - 1) in O(min(m, n)).

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.statementHtml:
Given n daily temperatures, for each day print the number of days you have to wait until a strictly warmer temperature appears. If there is no such future day, print 0 for that day. Output the n answers space-separated on a single line.

Input
Line 1: n.
Line 2: n integers.

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0])
temps = list(map(int, d[1:1+n]))
# TODO: print n integers, space-separated

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();int[] t=new int[n];for(int i=0;i<n;i++)t[i]=s.nextInt();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Sweep right-to-left, keeping a stack of indices whose answer hasn't been resolved.
Pop everything ≤ current temperature; the next remaining index is the warmer day.

problems.editorialHtml: Monotonic stack of indices with strictly decreasing temperatures. Each index is pushed/popped at most once → O(n).

solutions.code:
import sys
d=sys.stdin.read().split()
n=int(d[0]); t=list(map(int,d[1:1+n]))
ans=[0]*n; st=[]
for i,v in enumerate(t):
    while st and t[st[-1]]<v:
        j=st.pop(); ans[j]=i-j
    st.append(i)
print(' '.join(map(str,ans)))

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.statementHtml:
Given n integers and a target k, print the k most frequent elements space-separated, sorted by frequency descending. Break ties by element value ascending, so the answer is deterministic.

Input
Line 1: n and k.
Line 2: n integers.
problems.constraintsHtml: • 1 ≤ k ≤ number of distinct elements ≤ n ≤ 10^5

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0]); k = int(d[1])
arr = list(map(int, d[2:2+n]))
# TODO: print the top k frequent elements, space-separated

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt(),k=s.nextInt();long[] a=new long[n];for(int i=0;i<n;i++)a[i]=s.nextLong();/* TODO */}}

testCases.explanation: Tie between 4 and 5; tie-break by value asc → 4 first.

hints.text: Count frequencies, then sort items by (-freq, value) and take the first k.
Or use a size-k min-heap keyed on (freq, -value) for O(n log k).

problems.editorialHtml: Build a Counter, sort distinct items by (-frequency, value) and emit the first k keys. O(d log d) where d = distinct count.

solutions.code:
import sys
from collections import Counter
d=sys.stdin.read().split()
n=int(d[0]); k=int(d[1]); a=list(map(int,d[2:2+n]))
c=Counter(a)
items=sorted(c.items(), key=lambda kv:(-kv[1], kv[0]))
print(' '.join(str(it[0]) for it in items[:k]))

problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
problems.statementHtml:
You are given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1. Print the total amount of water that can be trapped after raining.

Input
Line 1: n.
Line 2: n non-negative integers — bar heights.

starters.code:
import sys
d = sys.stdin.read().split()
n = int(d[0])
h = list(map(int, d[1:1+n]))
# TODO: print the total trapped water

import java.util.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] g){Scanner s=new Scanner(System.in);int n=s.nextInt();long[] h=new long[n];for(int i=0;i<n;i++)h[i]=s.nextLong();/* TODO */}}

hints.text: Each bar traps min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height units, summed across all bars.
Compute it in one pass with two pointers: whichever side has the smaller max defines the trap on that side.

problems.editorialHtml: Two-pointer O(n) / O(1): walk from both ends, maintain left_max and right_max; advance the smaller side, adding the difference. Or precompute max arrays in O(n) / O(n).

problems.title: Employees Earning More Than Manager
problems.statementHtml: Table employees(id, name, salary, manager_id). Return the name of every employee whose salary is strictly greater than their manager's salary. Employees with no manager (manager_id IS NULL) are excluded. Order results by name ascending.

sql.solutionSql: SELECT e.name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary ORDER BY e.name;

hints.text: Self-join employees to themselves on manager_id = id, then filter on salary > m.salary.

problems.editorialHtml: SELECT e.name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary ORDER BY e.name;

problems.title: Top Earner Per Department
problems.statementHtml: Table employees(id, name, department, salary). For each department, return the department, the name of its top earner, and that salary. If multiple employees tie for the top salary in a department, you may assume the test data has a unique top earner per department. Order rows by department ascending.

sql.schemaSql:
CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, department TEXT, salary INTEGER);
INSERT INTO employees (id, name, department, salary) VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Eng',  90000),
  (2,'Bob',  'Eng',  85000),
  (3,'Carol','HR',   60000),
  (4,'Dan',  'HR',   70000),
  (5,'Eve',  'Sales',75000);
sql.solutionSql: SELECT department, name, salary FROM employees WHERE (department, salary) IN (SELECT department, MAX(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department) ORDER BY department;

hints.text: Find each department's max salary with GROUP BY, then match employees against that (department, salary) pair.
Window function alternative: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC).

problems.editorialHtml: Two-step pattern: aggregate max per group → match back. WHERE (department, salary) IN (SELECT department, MAX(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department) is the classic form.

problems.title: Running Total
problems.statementHtml: Table sales(id, day, amount). Return two columns — day and the cumulative total of amount through that day (aliased running) — ordered by day ascending. Use a window function.

sql.solutionSql: SELECT day, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS running FROM sales ORDER BY day;

hints.text: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day) computes the running total in a single scan.
Don't forget to alias the window expression AS running so the column name matches.

problems.editorialHtml: SELECT day, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS running FROM sales ORDER BY day;

problems.title: Second Highest Salary
problems.statementHtml: Table employees(id, name, salary). Return a single column named second_salary containing the second-highest distinct salary. If there is no such salary, return NULL.

sql.solutionSql: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_salary FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

hints.text: Take the MAX salary that is strictly less than the overall MAX.
Wrapping in MAX() lets MAX() of an empty set return NULL when no second salary exists.

problems.editorialHtml: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_salary FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees); — using MAX() ensures NULL when no second salary exists.
